result:
# The Weight of Ordinary Mornings

There's a particular kind of silence that exists at 5:47 AM, before the world remembers it has somewhere to be. I've been thinking about that silence lately — not as absence, but as presence. A room full of something we don't have adequate language for.

My grandmother used to say that the hours before dawn belonged to God and to fools, and she was never quite clear about which category she placed herself in as she stood at her kitchen counter, pressing her thumbs into bread dough while the neighborhood slept. I think about her hands often. Not the way they looked — I'm losing that, the specific visual memory eroding like coastline — but the way they moved. Certain and unhurried. She had solved something that I haven't solved yet.

We talk a lot about meaning. We build entire philosophies around it, write books, deliver lectures, argue in comment sections beneath articles we only half-read. But I wonder sometimes if meaning isn't the thing we should be chasing at all. I wonder if it's more like a cat — something that comes to you precisely when you stop calling for it, when you sit still long enough and turn your attention to something ordinary, like the way light moves across a wooden floor in the late afternoon, each minute shifting the geometry of shadow.

I've been wrong about a lot of things.

I spent years believing that the important moments in life would announce themselves — that they'd arrive with orchestral swells and a change in the quality of light, the way they do in films. That I would *know* when something was happening that mattered. But the moments that actually changed me came disguised as ordinary Tuesdays. A conversation in a parking lot. A sentence in a book I almost didn't read. The specific way someone laughed once, and how it rearranged something quietly inside my chest, a shifting of furniture in a room I didn't know I had.

Here is something I believe to be true: we are all carrying at least one story we've never told anyone. Not because it's dramatic or scandalous, but because we lack the architecture to hold it in language. It sits somewhere behind the sternum, this thing that happened or didn't happen, this feeling that arrived uninvited and never fully left. And we walk around with it. We go to the grocery store with it. We make small talk at parties with it. We fall asleep beside people we love, and it's there in the dark between us, not a wall exactly, but a weather system. Something with its own pressure and temperature.

I don't think this is tragedy. I think it might be the most human thing about us — this gap between experience and expression, the way we are each a country whose borders can be approached but never fully crossed. We try. Lord, we try. We write poems and songs and desperate 2 AM text messages. We press our bodies together. We look into each other's faces with a kind of hunger that has nothing to do with appetite. And we get close. Sometimes remarkably close. But there's always that last millimeter, that membrane-thin distance that reminds us we are, in the end, separate instruments trying to play the same note.

This isn't sad. Or maybe it is, but it's the kind of sadness that sits next to beauty so closely you can't tell where one ends and the other begins — like how the most gorgeous sunsets require pollution in the atmosphere, all that scattered light needing something broken to bend through.

My daughter asked me last week what I was most afraid of. She's seven, and she asked it the way seven-year-olds ask things — without preamble, while putting on her shoes, as though the question weighed no more than any other. I said something about spiders, which satisfied her. But the real answer, the one I kept in that space behind my sternum, is that I'm afraid of living carefully. Of arriving at the end having been so concerned with navigating correctly that I never looked up from the map to see the actual territory. The mountains. The weather. The way the road sometimes turns toward something you couldn't have planned for, something that would have been impossible to want because you didn't know it existed until you were standing in the middle of it.

I think about my grandmother's bread. How she never once used a recipe. How she'd adjust by feel — more water if the day was dry, a longer rise if the kitchen was cold. She was in conversation with the dough, responsive to conditions as they were, not as a recipe insisted they should be. There's a metaphor there that I'm not going to press too hard, because the best metaphors are the ones you let breathe.

Here is what I know at this point in my life, which is not much:

Be gentle. Not as a strategy, but as a practice. With others, yes, but also with yourself — with the version of you that got it wrong, that chose poorly, that lay awake at 3 AM reviewing a conversation from six years ago. That person was doing their best with what they had. Forgive them. Forgive them again tomorrow when you'll need to.

Pay attention to ordinary mornings. They are where most of your life actually lives.

And if someone you love is standing in a kitchen making something with their hands, watch them. Watch them carefully. Remember what you can.

You'll want it later.
